rationale: The citing paper uses Agrawal and Duhachek (2010) repeatedly in the introduction, theory positioning, and general discussion as the main prior evidence that guilt and shame appeals can trigger defensive processing and reduce persuasion. The 2012 paper explicitly contrasts its contribution against that result and develops a coping-fit account with gain/loss framing to explain when persuasion can increase instead. This is more than background citation because it directly structures the paper's research gap, hypotheses, and claimed contribution.
relation_description: The citing paper qualifies prior conclusions from Agrawal and Duhachek (2010) by showing boundary conditions: guilt and shame appeals become more effective when message framing fits emotion-specific coping tendencies. It reframes the earlier defensive-processing finding as contingent on message design rather than a uniform effect.
